title: vLLM Attention Surgeon
classificationReason: Structured attention pruning library with Python API and benchmarks
boardGateReason: High impact + confirmed feasibility from deep investigation
problemStatement: Large language models waste compute on redundant attention heads. Customers deploying self-hosted inference on OpenShift need lower latency and GPU memory usage without retraining models.
proposedPoc: Run attention-surgeon pruning on a Llama-3-8B checkpoint, quantize the result with LLM Compressor, and serve via vLLM on OpenShift AI. Measure TTFT and throughput vs. the unpruned baseline.
expectedOutcome: 15-25% memory reduction with <2% accuracy loss, proving the pruning-then-quantization pipeline works end-to-end on Red Hat AI.
customerRelevance: Enterprise teams running self-hosted inference on constrained GPU budgets benefit directly from model compression that requires no retraining.
justification: Directly extends Red Hat AI Inference Server capabilities with a structured pruning step before quantization.
risks: Requires specific PyTorch version alignment with vLLM
pmComments: asmith: Strong fit for inference optimization story

pmReviewHistory.comments: Strong fit for inference optimization story

title: Docling Serve
classificationReason: REST API server wrapping Docling document conversion
boardGateReason: High feasibility confirmed — Dockerfile and quickstart present
problemStatement: Enterprise RAG pipelines need a scalable document conversion service. Running Docling as a library couples conversion to the application process.
proposedPoc: Deploy docling-serve as a standalone service on OpenShift AI, connect it to a RAG ingestion pipeline, and measure conversion throughput across PDF, DOCX, and HTML inputs.
expectedOutcome: A production-ready document conversion microservice that integrates with Red Hat AI's RAG reference architecture.
customerRelevance: Every enterprise RAG deployment needs document ingestion — this turns Docling into a shared platform service.
justification: Extends the Docling story from library to deployable service, strengthening the data-ingest pillar.
pmComments: jdoe: Natural extension of Docling, deploy as shared service

pmReviewHistory.comments: Natural extension of Docling, deploy as shared service

title: AgentStack
classificationReason: CLI scaffolding tool for multi-agent applications
boardGateReason: Good developer experience angle for platform story
problemStatement: Developers building agent applications face a steep learning curve with orchestration frameworks, tool configuration, and deployment patterns.
proposedPoc: Use AgentStack to scaffold a multi-agent application, deploy it on OpenShift with Llama Stack as the backend, and compare developer time vs. manual setup.
expectedOutcome: A documented developer workflow that reduces agent app scaffolding from hours to minutes on Red Hat AI.
customerRelevance: Developer experience is a key differentiator — faster agent onboarding drives platform adoption.
justification: Validates the developer-experience pillar of the agentic AI strategy.
risks: Framework lock-in concerns; evaluate Llama Stack compatibility

title: TrustyAI Detoxify
classificationReason: Content safety guardrail plugin for TrustyAI
problemStatement: Enterprises deploying LLMs need content safety guardrails but existing solutions are cloud-locked or require complex integration.
proposedPoc: Deploy the detoxify guardrail as a TrustyAI plugin on OpenShift AI, wire it into a Llama Stack agent flow, and test against adversarial prompt datasets.
expectedOutcome: A self-hosted content safety layer that plugs into TrustyAI's guardrail framework without external API dependencies.
customerRelevance: Content safety is a top concern for regulated industries deploying AI — this provides an on-premise solution.
justification: Directly enriches TrustyAI's guardrail capabilities with a concrete content safety implementation.
pmComments: asmith: Perfect fit for TrustyAI story

title: KubeAI
boardGateReason: High duplicate risk with KServe — strategic overlap
problemStatement: Teams want simple model serving on Kubernetes without the full OpenShift AI operator overhead.
proposedPoc: Compare KubeAI deployment UX vs KServe on OpenShift AI for a simple inference workload.
expectedOutcome: Feature comparison document showing where KubeAI adds value vs existing KServe capabilities.
customerRelevance: Some customers prefer lightweight alternatives — understanding the gap helps position KServe.
justification: Competitive intelligence value even though it overlaps with existing capabilities.
pmComments: jdoe: Too much overlap with KServe, monitor only

pmReviewHistory.comments: Too much overlap with KServe, monitor only

title: RAGAS v0.3
classificationReason: RAG evaluation framework with automated test generation
boardGateReason: High impact; feasibility estimated from repo signals
problemStatement: Teams building RAG pipelines lack standardized evaluation metrics to compare retrieval quality across configurations.
proposedPoc: Integrate RAGAS v0.3 into an InstructLab evaluation pipeline on OpenShift AI, measuring faithfulness, relevancy, and context precision.
expectedOutcome: A repeatable RAG quality measurement workflow that complements InstructLab's existing evaluation capabilities.
customerRelevance: RAG quality assurance is a top ask from enterprises deploying knowledge-grounded AI systems.
justification: Fills the RAG evaluation gap in the model-customization pillar.
risks: Large dependency tree; v0.3 API may have breaking changes

title: OpenHands MCP Server
classificationReason: MCP server implementation for OpenHands coding agent
problemStatement: Coding agents need standardized tool protocols to connect to enterprise systems. MCP adoption needs concrete reference implementations.
proposedPoc: Deploy OpenHands with its MCP server on OpenShift, connect it to a Llama Stack backend, and demonstrate tool-calling across file, git, and terminal tools.
expectedOutcome: A working MCP reference implementation that validates the protocol on Red Hat AI infrastructure.
customerRelevance: MCP is becoming the standard protocol for agent tooling — demonstrating it on-platform builds credibility.
justification: Validates the MCP connectivity story with a practical coding agent use case.
blockers: Requires large model for best results
risks: High resource requirements; complex multi-container setup
pmComments: asmith: High potential but resource-heavy — revisit after GPU capacity review

pmReviewHistory.comments: High potential but resource-heavy — revisit after GPU capacity review

title: GPU Operator Autoscaler
boardGateReason: Below feasibility threshold — complex NVIDIA driver dependencies
problemStatement: GPU clusters serving multiple teams need dynamic scaling to avoid over-provisioning expensive accelerators.
proposedPoc: Install the GPU autoscaler alongside the NVIDIA GPU Operator on OpenShift and test scaling behavior under variable inference load.
expectedOutcome: Validated autoscaling workflow for GPU workloads on OpenShift, with cost savings metrics.
customerRelevance: GPU cost optimization is a universal concern for AI teams — autoscaling directly addresses it.
justification: Strengthens the GPU-as-a-Service operating model on OpenShift.
risks: Tight coupling to NVIDIA driver versions; may conflict with existing GPU operator configs

title: LitServe
classificationReason: Lightweight model serving framework with FastAPI integration
boardGateReason: Easy to deploy, useful for comparison benchmarks
problemStatement: Teams evaluating inference serving options need lightweight alternatives to compare against vLLM for non-LLM workloads.
proposedPoc: Deploy LitServe alongside vLLM on OpenShift AI, serve a vision model, and compare latency/throughput.
expectedOutcome: Benchmark data showing where lightweight serving fits vs full vLLM for non-LLM inference.
customerRelevance: Not all inference is LLM inference — customers with vision/audio models need serving options too.
justification: Benchmarking exercise to validate Red Hat AI Inference Server positioning.

title: Llama Stack Client SDK
classificationReason: Official Python client for Llama Stack APIs
boardGateReason: Core dependency for Llama Stack adoption
problemStatement: Developers building on Llama Stack need a stable, well-documented Python SDK to interact with the API programmatically.
proposedPoc: Build a sample agent application using the official SDK against a Llama Stack instance on OpenShift AI. Document the developer experience.
expectedOutcome: A reference application demonstrating SDK usage patterns for the most common Llama Stack workflows.
customerRelevance: SDK quality directly impacts developer adoption — a polished client accelerates time-to-first-agent.
justification: Foundation for all Python-based Llama Stack integrations on Red Hat AI.
risks: API surface may change as Llama Stack evolves
pmComments: jdoe: Core piece for Llama Stack story, must track closely

pmReviewHistory.comments: Core piece for Llama Stack story, must track closely

title: Llama Stack OpenShift Operator
classificationReason: Kubernetes operator for managing Llama Stack deployments
boardGateReason: High strategic relevance; feasibility estimated from repo signals
problemStatement: Deploying Llama Stack on OpenShift requires manual configuration of multiple components. An operator would automate lifecycle management.
proposedPoc: Test the operator's deployment workflow on OpenShift AI, validate it manages Llama Stack versions and scaling correctly.
expectedOutcome: Automated Llama Stack lifecycle management on OpenShift, reducing deployment friction.
customerRelevance: Operator-managed deployments are the OpenShift standard — customers expect this for production AI stacks.
justification: Critical for productizing Llama Stack on OpenShift AI.
risks: Early stage; API surface may change significantly

title: Model Context Protocol Spec
boardGateReason: Resource type — no direct POC, tracked for awareness
problemStatement: The MCP specification is evolving rapidly — tracking changes is essential for maintaining Llama Stack compatibility.
proposedPoc: Track specification changes and assess compatibility implications for Llama Stack's MCP implementation.
expectedOutcome: Up-to-date compatibility analysis ensuring Red Hat AI stays current with MCP evolution.
customerRelevance: Protocol conformance is a requirement for enterprise agent interoperability.
justification: Strategic awareness item for the MCP/agentic-ai pillar.
pmComments: jdoe: Track, don't POC — it's a spec not software

pmReviewHistory.comments: Track, don't POC — it's a spec not software

title: InstructLab Model Eval
classificationReason: Evaluation framework for InstructLab model training
problemStatement: InstructLab users need standardized evaluation to measure the quality of model customization before deployment.
proposedPoc: Run a full train-evaluate cycle using InstructLab eval on OpenShift AI, comparing model quality before and after SDG-based fine-tuning.
expectedOutcome: Documented evaluation workflow that integrates with the InstructLab training pipeline on Red Hat AI.
customerRelevance: Evaluation is the quality gate between training and deployment — customers need confidence in model quality.
justification: Completes the InstructLab story with a proper evaluation step.
pmComments: asmith: Essential InstructLab component, track actively

title: OCI Model Toolkit
classificationReason: CLI tools for packaging and signing ML models as OCI artifacts
boardGateReason: Clean Go CLI, well-documented, aligns with supply-chain strategy
problemStatement: ML models lack standardized packaging and provenance tracking — the OCI ecosystem has solved this for containers but not for models.
proposedPoc: Package a Llama model as an OCI artifact, sign it with cosign, push to a model registry, and deploy via KServe on OpenShift AI.
expectedOutcome: An end-to-end model supply chain workflow using OCI standards on Red Hat AI.
customerRelevance: Model provenance and signing are compliance requirements for regulated industries.
justification: Closes the model supply chain gap in the management pillar.
risks: OCI model spec is still maturing
